Advisor: Great. Income first: what’s your total gross income per year right now, all sources combined?

Advisor: Okay. And just so I understand the mix—what are the main sources? Salary versus investment income?
Client: Salary is 59300 annually, paid weekly. The rest is interest and dividends—95000 annually, and that comes in monthly.

Client: Salary is 59300 annually, paid weekly. The rest is interest and dividends—95000 annually, and that comes in monthly.

Advisor: When you say monthly expenses, does that include the mortgage payment or is that separate?
Client: Separate. The debt payment is 3100 a month—just the mortgage.

Client: Sure.

Client: Sure.
Advisor: Let’s move to assets. Roughly how much do you have in investable accounts total—so not counting the house value?

Client: Yeah. I’ve got 443650 in a taxable account on a retirement platform. Then 122800 in a 401k/IRA type account at a bank.

Client: There’s also 73950 in a bank-type account that’s on a retirement platform, and 76700 in private markets held at a bank.

Advisor: Alright, Michae—before we stop, I want to lock down a couple household basics so I don’t carry assumptions. What’s your marital status currently?

Advisor: Hi Michae—good to meet you. I’m glad you made the time. Before we dig into numbers, what would make this first meeting a win for you?
Client: Thanks. I’m Michae. I want to sanity-check retirement in about four years and make sure I’m not missing anything obvious—especially with the mortgage and healthcare stuff.
Advisor: Got it. Let’s keep it tight and practical. I’ll start with income and cash flow, then assets, then debts and insurance—sound okay?
Client: Yep, that works.

Client: Yes, US.
Advisor: Perfect. Last one in this bucket: when you think about your investing comfort level—ups and downs, staying the course—how would you describe your risk tolerance?
Client: Moderate.
Advisor: Thanks. And I know we already hit the big numbers—home value 288300, mortgage balance 210650 at 6.73%, payment 3100, payoff 2032-11-17, and the long-term care policy at 800 a month with 890400 coverage through 2036-04-21.
Client: Yep, that’s all correct.

Advisor: Totally hear you. Next time we’ll map your cash flow from now to age 63 and see what levers you actually have without overcomplicating it.
Client: Good. That’s what I’m looking for.
Advisor: Okay, Michae—before we wrap today, I want to make sure the debt picture is clean. Besides the mortgage, do you have any other debts at all? Credit cards, car loan, anything like that?
Client: No—non-mortgage debt outstanding is 0.
Advisor: Got it. And just to sanity-check, the only loan we’re tracking is the mortgage, right?
